chunks.page_summary: This document outlines the architecture and prerequisites for integrating Rheonics sensors with Node-RED and MQTT for remote data access and visualization using platforms like Ignition SCADA.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863
Integrating Rheonics Sensors with Node-RED and MQTT for Remote Data Access
Modified on: Thu, 18 Sep, 2025 at 4:57 PM

What is the purpose of this article?
This guide explains how to read data from a Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME) via Modbus TCP with Node-RED and publish it to an MQTT broker (HiveMQ in this example). The main goal is to enable remote visualization of sensor data from any location. This is achieved by using cloud-based MQTT brokers for accessible data exchange and combining it with Node-RED, which is a flexible and user-friendly tool to read, process, and publish sensor data into an MQTT broker.

TABLE OF CONTENTS
1. Prerequisites
2. Obtaining Data in Node-RED
   2.1. Open Node-RED
   2.2. Add a Modbus-Read Node
   2.3. Add a Function Node to Decode Float
   2.4. Add an MQTT-Out Node
   2.5. Deployment and Final Visualization of Node-RED flow
3. Resources

1. Prerequisites
1. Rheonics SME with Modbus TCP enabled
   SME connects to the sensor probe and transmits the data using different industrial communication protocols. Modbus TCP protocol is used in this article. (Link: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062)
2. Node-RED (https://nodered.org/) (Windows 10/11 or Linux)
   A visual programming tool used to collect, process, and route data. In this case, it acts as an intermediary that allows sending sensor data to an MQTT broker.
   node-red-contrib-modbus: A Node-RED add-on that enables communication with Modbus devices.
3. MQTT broker (HiveMQ in this article)
   A cloud-based MQTT message broker acts as an intermediary between clients that send (publish) and receive (subscribe) data. In this case, it transmits the sensor data received from Node-RED to Ignition for remote visualization.

Following this architecture, data from Rheonics sensors can be sent to an MQTT broker to visualize readings in any MQTT-compatible platform such as Ignition SCADA.

Figure 1: Architecture of Rheonics, Node-RED, MQTT, and Ignition integration

2. Obtaining Data in Node-RED
The goal is to obtain sensor data via Modbus TCP in Node-RED and send it to an MQTT broker. The completed Node-RED flow is shown below, and the following steps will explain how to build it.
key_facts: The article explains how to integrate Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME) with Node-RED and MQTT for remote data access.
The primary goal is to enable remote visualization of Rheonics sensor data from any location.
Data from Rheonics SME is read via Modbus TCP using Node-RED.
Node-RED is a flexible and user-friendly visual programming tool for reading, processing, and publishing sensor data.
MQTT brokers, such as HiveMQ, facilitate accessible cloud-based data exchange.
The integrated system allows sensor data to be visualized on any MQTT-compatible platform, with Ignition SCADA mentioned as an example.
A Node-RED add-on, node-red-contrib-modbus, is required for communication with Modbus devices.
diagram_labels: Rheonics Sensor (eg. SRV, SRD, DVP, others) for density, viscosity, temperature
Figure 1: Architecture of Rheonics, Node-RED, MQTT, and Ignition integration

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions on how to set up Node-RED, start it from a terminal, and add a Modbus-Read node to process sensor data via Modbus TCP for Rheonics sensors, demonstrating a flow from Modbus to MQTT.
chunks.extracted_text:
To learn the basic steps for setting up Node-RED and using the modules mentioned in this article, visit the Node-RED Setup and MQTT for Rheonics Sensors (https://support.rheonics.com)

Figure 2: Node-RED flow for sending data from Modbus to an MQTT

2.1. Open Node-RED
1. After installing Node-RED correctly, in a terminal, paste the following command to start running Node-RED:
node-red

2. The terminal will display the corresponding version and indicate that Node-RED is running correctly. The server URL will appear after the Server now running at, in this case, http://127.0.0.1:1880/ (http://127.0.0.1:1880/)

PS C:\Users> node-red

Figure 3: Node-RED running in a terminal

3. Paste the link in a browser to load the Node-RED editor.

Figure 4: Node-RED editor

2.2. Add a Modbus-Read Node
To obtain the sensors data through Modbus TCP, use the node-red-contrib-modbus module.
1. Drag a Modbus-read node onto the flow.
key_facts: Node-RED can be used to send data from Modbus to MQTT.
Rheonics Sensors data can be obtained through Modbus TCP using the `node-red-contrib-modbus` module.
Node-RED is started by typing `node-red` in the terminal.
The Node-RED server typically runs locally at `http://127.0.0.1:1880/`.
The Node-RED version shown in the terminal output is `v4.0.9`, and Node.js version is `v22.17.1`.
The operating system identified from the terminal output is `Windows_NT 10.0.26100 x64 LE`.
Node-RED flow credentials are encrypted using a system-generated key by default.
Users are advised to set their own `credentialSecret` in the Node-RED settings file to re-encrypt credentials and prevent loss if the system-generated key is lost.
warnings_notes: Projects disabled : editorTheme.projects.enabled=false (from Node-RED terminal output)
Flows file : \Users\...\.node-red\flows.json (from Node-RED terminal output, indicated as a warning)

Your flow credentials file is encrypted using a system-generated key. If the system-generated key is lost for any reason, your credentials file will not be recoverable, you will have to delete it and re-enter your credentials.
You should set your own key using the 'credentialSecret' option in your settings file. Node-RED will then re-encrypt your credentials file using your chosen key the next time you deploy a change.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for configuring Modbus-Read nodes and Modbus TCP clients to acquire and decode viscosity data from a Rheonics SME device.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 5: Modbus-Read node

2. Double-click to configure:
  * Name: Viscosity (It is just the label of the node)
  * Function Code: FC4 - Read Input Registers
  * Address: 40 (viscosity register). The rest of the input parameters can be found in Modbus TCP - Input Registers
    (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664)
  * Quantity: 2 (float = 2 registers)
  * Poll Rate: 3s (depends on frequency of data reading desired)

Figure 6: Modbus-Read Node Configuration

3. Click on the Server field and press the plus to add a new Modbus TCP client. In this case, it corresponds to the SME:
  * Type: TCP
  * Host: IP address of the SME.
    (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297604-connecting-the-modbus-tcp-outputs)
  * Port: 502 (default)
  * Unit ID: 255

Figure 7: Modbus Client Configuration

2.3. Add a Function Node to Decode Float
The Modbus-Read node returns the values of the registers, which need to be transformed to obtain the value of viscosity. Add a function node to convert the 2 16-bit registers into a float value.

Figure 8: Function node

1. Drag a function node onto the flow.
2. Double-click on the node and add the following code to the On Message section. This allows to return the viscosity as a float.
key_facts: The Modbus-Read node is configured to read input registers for viscosity data.
Viscosity values are stored across two 16-bit Modbus registers and must be converted to a float.
The poll rate of the Modbus-Read node can be adjusted based on the desired data reading frequency.
A Modbus TCP client must be added and configured to communicate with the SME device.
The default Modbus TCP port is 502.
A function node is required to transform the raw register values from the Modbus-Read node into a usable float viscosity value.
warnings_notes: The 'Name' field for the Modbus-Read node (e.g., Viscosity) is solely a label for the node.
Address '40' specifically refers to the viscosity register.
A float value requires 2 Modbus registers for its representation.
The poll rate should be set according to the desired data reading frequency.
Port 502 is the default setting for Modbus TCP communication.
The values returned by the Modbus-Read node are raw registers and require transformation into a float to represent viscosity.

chunks.page_summary: This page details the configuration of an MQTT-out node for publishing Rheonics sensor data, specifically viscosity, to a HiveMQ broker, including a JavaScript function for float conversion.
key_facts: An MQTT-out node is used to publish sensor data to a broker.
The HiveMQ broker is utilized in this example for data publishing.
Rheonics sensor data, specifically viscosity, is published under the MQTT topic "srv/viscosity".
MQTT Quality of Service (QoS) level 1 ensures that messages are received by the broker at least once.

A JavaScript function node performs float conversion by writing two 16-bit unsigned integers (Big-Endian) from the message payload into a 4-byte buffer and then reading a 32-bit floating-point number (Big-Endian) from that buffer.
Configuration for the HiveMQ broker integration with Rheonics is available at the provided support URL.
diagram_labels: Tip: Leave topic, qos or retain blank if you want to set them via msg properties.
warnings_notes: Tip: Leave topic, qos or retain blank if you want to set them via msg properties.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for configuring an MQTT broker (HiveMQ) and deploying a Node-RED flow, detailing connection parameters, security settings, and the final deployment action.
chunks.extracted_text:
4. Next, the properties of the MQTT broker are filled as follow:
*   Name: Desired name for the broker
*   Server: HiveMQ Connection URL
*   Port: Port used by the broker
*   Use TLS: Enable if using encrypted communication
*   Protocol: MQTT V5

Figure 12: MQTT broker addition
Tip: Leave topic, qos or retain blank if you want to set them via msg properties

Figure 13: HiveMQ Broker Configuration - Connection Tab

5. Go to the Security Tab on the HiveMQ Broker configuration and fill it with the credentials of the HiveMQ broker.
	1.  Username: username credentials in HiveMQ broker
	2.  Password: password credentials in HiveMQ broker

Figure 14: HiveMQ Broker Configuration - SecurityTab

2.5. Deployment and Final Visualization of Node-RED flow
1.  After adding correctly and configuring the nodes, press on the Deploy button to run the flow.

Figure 15: Deployment of Node-RED flow

2. The final flow looks as follow.
key_facts: The MQTT broker requires configuration of its name, server URL, port, TLS usage, and protocol.
HiveMQ is specified as the MQTT broker.
The connection topic for the MQTT broker is set to 'srv/viscosity'.
The MQTT broker configuration includes options for automatic connection, TLS encryption, and a keep-alive interval of 60 seconds.
Broker credentials, including username and password, are configured under the 'Security' tab of the HiveMQ Broker settings.
A Node-RED flow is executed by pressing the 'Deploy' button after all nodes are configured.
diagram_labels: Figure 13: HiveMQ Broker Configuration - Connection Tab
warnings_notes: Tip: Leave topic, qos or retain blank if you want to set them via msg properties

3. Repeat the same procedure to obtain other parameters of the SME by modifying the address and topic. (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418655)

To learn how to integrate Ignition SCADA with Rheonics sensors via MQTT refer to Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics Sensor Data via MQTT (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418655)

key_facts: The document illustrates a complete Node-RED flow for Modbus to MQTT conversion.
Users can obtain additional parameters from the SME by adjusting the Modbus address and topic.
Integration of Rheonics sensors with Ignition SCADA using MQTT is a key topic addressed with a specific resource link.
A section titled 'Resources' provides links to support articles and general information regarding Modbus TCP, Node-RED, MQTT, and HiveMQ integration with Rheonics sensors.
